pattern: Converting every point to its bearing angle from the viewer turns 'find a rotation that covers the most points within a fixed field of view' into a fixed-width sliding window over sorted angles, with the list duplicated past 360 degrees so a window is free to wrap across the seam, plus a separate tally for points sitting exactly on the viewer.
hints: Points located exactly at your own position are visible no matter how you rotate, so pull those out and count them separately from everything else.
For every remaining point, compute the bearing angle from your position to it and sort those angles; the widest field of view you can rotate to becomes a fixed-size window over this sorted list.

Because the field of view can straddle the 0/360-degree seam, append every angle again with 360 added and slide a window over the doubled list; take the largest window whose span is at most the given field of view, then add back the points at your exact position. Rounding each angle to a handful of decimal places before comparing avoids floating-point noise from atan2 flipping a borderline case.

edge_inputs.description: statement example two, includes a point at the viewer's own location
statement example three, narrow field of view
a zero-degree field of view exactly aligned with the only other point
field of view at the maximum allowed value, four cardinal points around the viewer
two points whose bearings sit just on either side of the 0/360 seam, only visible together if the window is allowed to wrap
duplicate points exactly at the viewer's location mixed with one ordinary point
one point in each of the four cardinal directions from the viewer, exercising every atan2 quadrant
points at the maximum coordinate bound around a mid-board viewer

stress.description: random points and viewer scattered across the whole board with a handful of points placed exactly on the viewer
points clustered near a single bearing with small perturbations, stressing angle-rounding boundary comparisons
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 150))
    loc = [50, 50]
    points = []
    for _ in range(n):
        y = max(0, min(100, 50 + rng.randint(-2, 2)))
        x = rng.randint(51, 100)
        points.append([x, y])
    angle = rng.randint(0, 20)
    return [points, angle, loc]

solutions.python:
import math

class Solution:
    def visiblePoints(self, points, angle: int, location) -> int:
        px, py = location
        same = 0
        angles = []
        for x, y in points:
            dx, dy = x - px, y - py
            if dx == 0 and dy == 0:
                same += 1
                continue
            a = math.degrees(math.atan2(dy, dx))
            if a < 0:
                a += 360
            angles.append(round(a, 6))
        angles.sort()
        n = len(angles)
        if n == 0:
            return same
        extended = angles + [a + 360 for a in angles]
        eps = 1e-6
        best = 0
        j = 0
        for i in range(n):
            while j < i + n and extended[j] - angles[i] <= angle + eps:
                j += 1
            best = max(best, j - i)
        return best + same

complexity.time: O(n log n) for the angle sort, O(n) for the sliding window
